items.passage: Throughout the long history of life, several mass extinctions have wiped out the majority of species in a relatively short span of time. Fossils preserved in rock layers reveal that these dramatic losses were sometimes followed by bursts of new diversity. Scientists link certain extinctions to sudden events such as massive volcanic eruptions or the impact of a large asteroid. Such disasters can alter the global climate and disrupt the food chains that countless organisms depend upon. By studying these patterns, researchers learn how ecosystems eventually recover after enormous environmental shocks.

Catalysts are remarkable substances that speed up chemical reactions without being consumed or permanently changed in the process. They work by offering an easier pathway that requires less energy for the reacting molecules to combine. Because a single catalyst can be used again and again, even tiny amounts can transform large quantities of material. Many industries rely on them to manufacture fuels, plastics, and fertilizers quickly and cheaply. Living cells contain natural catalysts called enzymes, which guide the countless reactions that keep an organism alive and functioning.

Beneath the floor of a healthy forest lies a vast hidden web of fungal threads connecting the roots of many trees. These delicate filaments exchange water and nutrients with plants in return for sugars produced during photosynthesis. Through this partnership, a tall tree can quietly share resources with smaller neighbors growing in the shade. Some scientists believe the network even carries chemical signals that warn distant plants about insects or disease. Such cooperation suggests that a forest behaves less like separate individuals and more like one connected community.

Broken fragments of pottery are among the most valuable clues that archaeologists recover from ancient settlements. Because styles of decoration changed steadily over the centuries, the shape and pattern of a vessel can reveal its approximate age. A method called thermoluminescence measures the faint light released when heated clay is warmed again in the laboratory. This signal grows stronger the longer a pot has rested in the ground since it was originally fired. By combining such techniques, experts reconstruct trade routes and daily customs of communities that left no written records.

Friction is the resisting force that arises whenever two surfaces slide or attempt to slide against each other. It depends on how tightly the surfaces press together and on the roughness of the materials involved. Although friction often wastes energy as heat, it also makes ordinary actions like walking and gripping possible. Engineers reduce unwanted friction with smooth coatings and oils that allow moving parts to glide more freely. Without this force, objects set in motion would never slow down and vehicles could not safely start or stop.

Stars are born inside enormous clouds of gas and dust known as nebulae, scattered throughout every galaxy. Within these cold regions, gravity slowly pulls scattered particles together into dense clumps over millions of years. As a clump collapses, its center grows hotter and denser until nuclear reactions finally ignite and produce light. The fierce energy released then pushes away the surrounding material, revealing a brilliant new star to the universe. Leftover dust circling the young star may later gather into planets, moons, and the smaller bodies around them.
